zh.lifeSummary: 當Wilma Elizabeth Wilmont Thompson Marshall of York於1575年出生在英國越可爾時，她的父親Richarde Marshall of York25歲，母親Anne Thompson20歲。她與William Judsonne在英國英格蘭結婚。他們至少是2個兒子和1個女兒的父母。她去世於1665年8月29日在英國越可爾利德斯利德斯 明斯特享年90歲。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Wilma Elizabeth Wilmont Thompson Marshall of York于1575年出生在英国越可爾时，她的父亲Richarde Marshall of York25岁，母亲Anne Thompson20岁。她与William Judsonne在英国英格兰结婚。他们至少是2个儿子和1个女儿的父母。她去世于1665年8月29日在英国越可爾利德斯利德斯 明斯特享年90岁。

nameMeanings.name: Wilmont
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of Walloon or English Wilmot . The surname Wilmont is also found in Belgium (Wallonia) and northern France, but it is very rare there.
In some cases probably also Dutch: altered form of Walloon Wilmot or of its very rare French cognate Willemont.

variants.name: Wilmot
meanings: English: from the Middle English personal name Will(i)mot(t), a hypocoristic form of Willelm (see William ). Compare the Old French personal name Guillemot. This surname has been established in Ireland (Kerry) since 1614.
Walloon: from a pet form of the personal name   William  . Compare   Wilmont  .
History: Benjamin Wilmot and his wife, with their 6-year-old son William, emigrated from England to New Haven, CT, in or before 1640.

nameMeanings.name: Thompson
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: patronymic from the Middle English personal name T(h)om(me) (see Thom ) + -son ‘son of Tom’. Thomson is usually the Scottish form, that with the intrusive -p- being English. Both forms are common in Ireland. The surname Thompson is also very common among African Americans.
Americanized form of Danish, Norwegian, and North German Thomsen and of its Swedish cognate Thomsson. Compare Thomson .

variants.name: Thomson
meanings: Scottish, English and northern Irish: variant of   Thompson  .
Scottish: sometimes an Anglicized form of Gaelic McThomas; see McTavish , McComish .
Americanized form of Danish, Norwegian, and North German   Thomsen  . Compare   Thompson  .

variants.name: Thom
meanings: Scottish and English: from the Middle English personal name T(h)om(me), a pet form of Thomas . In Scotland, Thom is also an Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Thom, see McComb .
German: from the personal name Thom, a pet form of Thomas . This surname is also found in France.
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 譚, see   Tan   1.

variants.name: Tomson
meanings: English: variant of   Thompson  .

variants.name: Thompsom
meanings: Altered form of   Thompson  .

variants.name: Tompson
meanings: English: variant of   Thompson  .

nameMeanings.name: Marshall
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: usually an occupational name ‘farrier’, occasionally a status name ‘chief official of a royal household or court; a high officer of state’, from Middle English mareshal and Old French maresc(h)al. An even wider range of meanings is found in some other languages: compare for example Polish Marszałek (see Marszalek ). This name has been established in Ireland since the 13th century. It is also borne by Jews, presumably as an Americanized form of one or more similar (like-sounding) Jewish surnames.
Americanized form of German   Marschall  .
Americanized form of French   Mercier  .

variants.name: Marchel
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Jacek, Ryszard.  German Ernst.

Polish: probably from French maréchal or Old French maresc(h)al, which was originally an occupational name for someone who was in charge of the horses of a household, later a military rank for a senior general. See Marshall and compare Marszalek .

variants.name: Mercier
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Marcel, Andre, Pierre, Gaston, Lucien, Aurele, Jacques, Normand, Laurent, Michel, Valmont.

French and Channel Islands (Jersey): occupational name for a merchant, from Old French mercier, from Late Latin merciarius (an agent derivative of merx, genitive mercis ‘merchandise’). This name was also taken to England by Huguenot refugees. Compare Marshall and Mercy .

variants.name: Maniscalco
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Vito, Sal, Antonio, Ignazio, Carmelo, Francesco, Gaspare, Saverio, Antonino, Carlo, Damiano.

Italian (southern): occupational name from maniscalco ‘farrier’, altered from marescalco by folk etymology, probably influenced by mano ‘hand’, from medieval Latin mariscalcus, marescalcus. Compare Marshall .

variants.name: Marchal
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Gilles, Jacques, Maryse, Philippe, Pierre, Veronique.

French and Walloon: status name or occupational name from Old French maresc(h)al ‘marshal’. The term is of ancient Germanic origin (from marah ‘horse, mare’ + scalc ‘servant’) and was originally applied to a man who looked after horses. In the Middle Ages it also came to be used on the one hand as an occupational name for a farrier, and on the other as a status name for an officer of state, in particular a member of a royal household with military responsibilities. Compare English Marshall and 2 below.
English: variant of   Marshall  . This form of the surname is rare in Britain and Ireland, and is found mainly in Glamorgan, Wales.

variants.name: Maskell
meanings: English: variant of Marshall , derived from Middle English, Old French maresc(h)al ‘marshal’. The first element in the placename Mascallsbury (Essex) derives from the surname of a 13th-century individual named Robert le Marescal.

variants.name: Marszalek
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Piotr, Alicja, Andrzej, Eugeniusz, Henryk, Jerzy, Kazimierz, Mieczyslaw, Wieslaw, Zbigniew, Zofia, Zygmunt.

Polish (Marszałek): occupational or status name from the vocabulary word marszałek ‘marshal’, denoting anything from a high court official, a field marshal, or the chairman of the Polish parliament, to the senior servant in a great household. Compare English Marshall .

variants.name: Marshell
meanings: English: variant of   Marshall  .

variants.name: Marschall
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Erwin, Hedwig, Juergen, Reinhard, Theresia, Wilfried.

German: occupational name for a man in the service of a member of the nobility, originally one who looked after the horses, Middle High German marschalc (from mar(c) ‘noble horse’ + schalk ‘servant’). In time the term came to be used for a military rank or a certain court official. Compare Marshall .
English: variant of   Marshall  . This form of the surname is rare in Britain and Ireland.

variants.name: Marshal
meanings: English and Scottish: variant of   Marshall  .

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Korean Kyong, Kyong Suk, Young, Chae, Chi Young, Hang, Kil, Rin, Sang Hun, Chang, Chong, Insoo, Kum, Sun Young, Yom, Young Hwan.  Cambodian Kiwan, Oeurn, Phoeun, Pich, Saroeuth, Seung, Sophath, Sophea, Yoeun, Phan, Son.  Indian Shanti, Harendra.
Indian (Uttar Pradesh): Brahmin name, from Sanskrit om, the Hindu sacred and mystic syllable uttered at the beginning of prayers.
Cambodian: written ឱម, corresponding to the exclamation of solemn affirmation, of Sanskrit origin (see 1 above), chanted by Buddhist monks.

variants.name: Oum
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Southeast Asian Heang, Hye, Kam, Kan, Kheng, Khin, Kun, Sang, Sok, Phong, Yom, Yoo, Youn, Cuong, Phal, Samnang, Tin.  Cambodian Sokha, Chheang, Choeun, Moeun, Roeun, Sambath, Samoeun, Savuth, Soeun, Sopheap.
Cambodian: written អ៊ុំ, from a Khmer word denoting an elder sibling of one's parent (i.e. ‘uncle’ or ‘aunt’). Compare   Om   3 and   Um   2.

nameMeanings.name: York
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from the city of York in northern England. The surname is now widespread throughout England. Originally, the city bore the Latin name Eburacum, which is probably from a Brittonic name meaning ‘yew-tree place’. This was altered by folk etymology to Old English Eoforwīc (from the elements eofor ‘wild boar’ + wīc ‘specialized farmstead’). This name was taken over by Scandinavian settlers, who altered it back to opacity in the form Jórvík or Jórk (English York, which became finally settled as the placename in the 13th century). The surname has also been adopted by Jews as an Americanized form of various like-sounding Jewish surnames.
In some cases also an American shortened and altered form of the East Slavic patronymic   Yurkovich   or its Croatian, Slovak, or Slovenian variants. Compare   Yurk  .

variants.name: Yurkovich
meanings: Ukrainian (standard transliteration Yurkovych) and Belorussian: patronymic from the personal name Yurko and its Belorussian equivalent Yurka, pet forms of Ukrainian Yuriy and Belorussian Yuray, equivalents of Greek Geōrgios (see George ).
Americanized form of Croatian Jurković, Slovak and Slovenian Jurkovič (see Jurkovic , compare Jurkovich and Yurkovic ).
Jewish (from Bessarabia): habitational name for someone from Yurkovtsy in Ukraine.

variants.name: Yorke
meanings: English: variant of   York  .

variants.name: Yorks
meanings: English: variant of   York  .

nameMeanings.name: Wilma
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Contracted form of   Wilhelmina  , which has retained rather more currency (especially in America) than the full form of the name.

nameMeanings.name: Elizabeth
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: The usual spelling of   Elisabeth   in English. It is recorded in the medieval period, but was made popular by being borne by  Queen Elizabeth I  of England (  1533–1603 ). In the 20th century it again became extremely fashionable, partly because it was the name of  Elizabeth Bowes-Lyon  (  1900–2002 ), who in  1936  became Queen Elizabeth as the wife of King George VI, and after his death in  1952  achieved great public affection as Queen Mother for nearly half a century. Even more influentially, it is the name of her daughter  Queen Elizabeth II  (  b. 1926 ).

variants.name: Elisabeth
meanings: The spelling of Elizabeth used in the Authorized Version of the New Testament, and in most modern European languages. This was the name of the mother of John the Baptist (Luke 1:60). Etymologically, the name means ‘God is my oath’, and is therefore identical with Elisheba, the name of the wife of Aaron according to the genealogy at Exodus 6:23. The final element seems to have been altered by association with Hebrew shabbāth ‘Sabbath’.

historicEvents.title: The First English Colony is Established in America
historicEvents.description: England established its first colony in North America in 1585 as a way to generate additional wealth. The colony was named Virginia, after the virgin Queen Elizabeth I.

historicEvents.title: Elizabeth I Signs Death Warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots
historicEvents.description: Elizabeth I signed the death warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots, on February 1, 1587, as she was found guilty of plotting to assasinate Elizabeth. Mary, Queen of Scots, was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle in England on February 8, 1587.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Elizabeth I.org - Mary Queen of Scots

historicEvents.title: The Honourable East India Company
historicEvents.description: The Honourable East India Company, a British joint-stock company, was established in 1600 for trade in the Indian Ocean region. At its height, the British East India Company had a private army which was twice the size of the British Army, ruled large sections of India, and revenues in the millions.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Historic UK - The East India Company

historicEvents.title: The Pilgrims Board the Mayflower
historicEvents.description: "The Pilgrims boarded the Mayflower in search of religious freedom in the New World in September 1620. The Mayflower set sail from Plymouth, England, with 102 passengers, 40 of whom called themselves ""Saints."" After two stormy months at sea, the ship reached the New World. Nearly half of the group died during their first winter due to malnutrition and the harsh New England winter."

historicEvents.title: The English Civil War
historicEvents.description: A series of conflicts regarding England's governance during the years 1642 to 1651 is now known as The English Civil War. Charles I summoned supporters to join him against his enemies in Parliament. In October 1642, nearly 10,000 men fought for Charles I and chased Parliament across the River Tamar. Fighting continued for years and was finally ended at the Battle of Worcester on September 3, 1651, with a Parliamentarian victory.

en.lifeSummary: When Wilma Elizabeth Wilmont Thompson Marshall of York was born in 1575, in Yorkshire, England, her father, Richarde Marshall of York, was 25 and her mother, Anne Thompson, was 20. She married William Judsonne in England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 1 daughter. She died on 29 August 1665, in Leeds Minster, Leeds, Yorkshire, England, at the age of 90.

Elle a épousé William Judsonne à Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 2 fils et 1 fille. Elle est décédée le 29 août 1665, à Leeds Minster, Leeds, Everwyck, Angleterre, à l’âge de 90 ans.

ko.lifeSummary: Wilma Elizabeth Wilmont Thompson Marshall of York(이)가 1575년 영국 요르크실르에서 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 Richarde Marshall of York(은)는 25세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Anne Thompson(은)는 20세였다. 그녀는 영국 잉글랜드에서 William Judsonne(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그녀는 1665년 8월 29일 영국 요르크실르 리드즈 리드즈 민스터에서 90세의 나이로 사망했다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Wilma Elizabeth Wilmont Thompson Marshall of York родилась в 1575 г. в местности еркшир, Англия, её отцу по имени Richarde Marshall of York было 25 лет, а матери, которую звали Anne Thompson, 20. Она вышла замуж за человека по имени William Judsonne в местности Англия, Великобритания. У них было по крайней мере 2 сына и 1 дочь. Она умерла 29 августа 1665 г. в местности лидс минстир, лидс, еркшир, Англия в возрасте 90 лет.

Se casó con William Judsonne en Inglaterra, Reino Unido. Fueron padres de por lo menos 2 hijos y 1 hija. Murió el 29 de agosto de 1665, en Leeds Minster, Leeds, Yorkshire, Inglaterra, a la edad de 90 años.
